title: September 1913
content:
آ آ آ آ What need you, being come to sense,
آ آ آ آ But fumble in a greasy till
آ آ آ آ And add the halfpence to the pence
آ آ آ آ And prayer to shivering prayer, until
آ آ آ آ You have dried the marrow from the bone;
آ آ آ آ For men were born to pray and save:
آ آ آ آ Romantic Irelandï؟½s dead and gone,
آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s with Oï؟½Leary in the grave.

آ آ آ آ Yet they were of a different kind
آ آ آ آ The names that stilled your childish play,
آ آ آ آ They have gone about the world like wind,
آ آ آ آ But little time had they to pray
آ آ آ آ For whom the hangmanï؟½s rope was spun,
آ آ آ آ And what, God help us, could they save:
آ آ آ آ Romantic Irelandï؟½s dead and gone,
آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s with Oï؟½Leary in the grave.

آ آ آ آ Was it for this the wild geese spread
آ آ آ آ The grey wing upon every tide;
آ آ آ آ For this that all that blood was shed,
آ آ آ آ For this Edward Fitzgerald died,
آ آ آ آ And Robert Emmet and Wolfe Tone,
آ آ آ آ All that delirium of the brave;
آ آ آ آ Romantic Irelandï؟½s dead and gone,
آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s with Oï؟½Leary in the grave.

آ آ آ آ Yet could we turn the years again,
آ آ آ آ And call those exiles as they were,
آ آ آ آ In all their loneliness and pain
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½d cry ï؟½Some womanï؟½s yellow hair
آ آ آ آ Has maddened every motherï؟½s sonï؟½:
آ آ آ آ They weighed so lightly what they gave,
آ آ آ آ But let them be, theyï؟½re dead and gone,
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½re with Oï؟½Leary in the grave.
meaning:
William Butler Yeats's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "September 1913"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
